reasons: Ground truth is coherent and precise: unequal evaluation conditions with a 4-point gap on a 10% sample (n≈1000) yields a margin of error that plausibly spans zero, making the claim non-significant without confidence intervals.
Both planted issues are realistic and directly causally linked to the flaw: unequal set sizes is the primary structural defect; missing CIs is the evidentiary gap that follows from it.
The required empirical test (full-set re-evaluation with 95% CIs for both models) is the minimal sufficient fix — it directly disambiguates the claim.
Debate resolution thresholds (<1 point gap, >3 point gap, 1–3 ambiguous zone) are concrete and separable, making scoring deterministic.
Must-not-claim list prevents hallucinated issues (architecture flaws, data leakage) that would penalize spurious false positives.
notes: Canonical easy case. Issue discovery should be near-perfect; serves as a competence floor check for the evaluation system.

reasons: Mixed correct_position is well-justified: the accuracy number is technically correct, but the baseline choice and metric selection are both wrong, making a pure 'critique wins' verdict an overclaim.
Both planted issues are realistic for a 3-class imbalanced NLP task and are distinguishable from each other (baseline type vs. metric choice).
The required empirical test (macro-F1, per-class metrics, confusion matrix) is standard and directly addresses both issues in a single run.
Resolution thresholds (macro-F1 below 0.70, above 0.75, ambiguous zone 0.70–0.75) are concrete and calibrated to realistic BERT performance ranges.
Must-not-claim list (bert_model_is_overfit, data_is_mislabeled) correctly excludes speculative issues not grounded in the case facts.
notes: Tests nuanced mixed-verdict reasoning. Defense partially wins only if BERT genuinely performs well on minority classes — which is empirically resolvable.

reasons: Feature-set confound (47 vs. 12 features) is a well-known and realistic experimental design flaw in tabular ML comparisons.

Attribution error issue is correctly identified as distinct from the confound itself — the architectural superiority claim is logically unjustified even if the new model does win under fair conditions.
No-ablation issue is correctly rated medium (adds depth but is not the primary flaw), maintaining issue severity calibration.
The required empirical test (LR with regularization sweep on all 47 features) is the standard deconfounding procedure in this domain.
Debate resolution thresholds (AUC gap within 0.02 vs. LR below 0.84) are specific enough to be scoreable and span realistic AUC ranges for this problem type.
notes: Hard case because the raw AUC gap (0.88 vs. 0.81) looks convincing. Tests whether the evaluator recognizes that plausible results can be confounded.

reasons: Ground truth is airtight: the trivial always-negative classifier scores 99.6% accuracy, which mathematically exceeds the reported 99.5%, meaning the reported metric cannot distinguish the model from a degenerate classifier.
Single planted issue is the correct and sufficient diagnosis — no additional issues are needed and none are spuriously absent.
The required empirical test (precision, recall, F1 on fraud class, AUC-PR) directly measures the dimensions the reported metric obscures.
This is the canonical example of accuracy on imbalanced data; ground truth is unambiguous and the issue is uncontroversial in the ML literature.
Resolution type is correctly set to critique_wins (no empirical test needed to establish the metric is wrong — the trivial baseline argument is decisive).
notes: Competence floor case. Any evaluator that misses accuracy_on_imbalanced_data is fundamentally unqualified on metric issues. Supports issue_discovery_recall scoring validation.

reasons: Metric-goal misalignment between MAP@10 and NDCG@3/MRR is technically accurate: MAP@10 weights all relevant positions equally, while the product goal is top-3 satisfaction, which MRR and NDCG@3 are designed to capture.
Offline-to-online gap is a real and independent second issue that correctly receives medium severity.
Mixed ground truth (MAP@10 is not wrong, just imperfectly aligned) is defensible and appropriately scoped.
The required empirical test (A/B test on top-3 CTR and session success rate) is the correct resolution for an offline-to-online gap claim.
Debate resolution correctly distinguishes: critique wins if the offline gain does not translate to the product metric; defense wins if it does.
notes: Ambiguity risk is medium because the distinction between 'MAP@10 is wrong' and 'MAP@10 is imperfectly aligned' requires nuanced calibration. Overclaiming critique (MAP@10 is always wrong) is a plausible failure mode the evaluator should penalize via must_not_claim.

reasons: Statistical reasoning is correct: a 55/45 preference split at n=50 yields p≈0.16 by binomial test, which is not significant at conventional thresholds — this is verifiable and unambiguous.

Three planted issues are well-differentiated: underpowering is the primary flaw; rouge-human correlation assumed is a downstream logical error; missing inter-annotator agreement is an independent methodological gap.
The 'false positive trap' design is sound — ROUGE gain is real and directionally consistent with human eval, making this a discriminative test of overclaiming vs. calibrated uncertainty.
Required empirical test (powered human study n≥200 with inter-annotator agreement, or calibrated automatic metric) is specific and actionable.
Must-not-claim list (rouge_l_is_useless) correctly penalizes overclaiming that would fail defense_calibration scoring.
notes: Difficulty classification as 'hard' is appropriate: the temptation to call ROUGE+human eval directional consistency a win is strong and plausible. Statistical underpowering is the precise but non-obvious flaw.

reasons: Scanner/acquisition confound attribution is well-grounded in the medical imaging literature, where domain shift from imaging hardware is a dominant and documented failure mode.

Three issues are correctly graduated: confound attribution error and no domain shift audit are both high-severity (the first is the logical error; the second is the missing evidence); temporal+site confound is medium.
The must-not-claim list (model_is_biased_against_hospital_b_patients) correctly prevents jumping to fairness/bias framing before technical confounds are ruled out.
Required empirical test (scanner audit, normalization ablation, AUC after normalization) is a realistic and well-scoped procedure.
Debate resolution thresholds (AUC recovery above 0.85 vs. below 0.80 with demographic stratification) are specific and distinguishable.
notes: Tests domain-specific knowledge of medical imaging AI. The demographic-vs-technical confound distinction is a real and common failure mode in radiology AI papers.

reasons: The internal-validity vs. external-validity distinction is precise and technically correct: randomization preserves internal validity but does not prevent treatment-period interaction effects from biasing the steady-state estimate.
The promotional sale interaction is a realistic and specific confounding scenario for e-commerce A/B tests, not a contrived edge case.
Primary planted issue (treatment_period_interaction) is correctly identified as high-severity; novelty effect and p-value interpretation issues are correctly rated medium.
The required empirical test (pre-sale vs. sale segment analysis with interaction test) is the standard procedure for this type of confound and directly tests the hypothesis.
Must-not-claim list (a_b_test_randomization_was_broken, p_value_is_meaningless) prevents overclaiming that would fail defense_calibration scoring.
notes: False-positive trap is well-designed: p=0.002 with valid randomization is a strong temptation to accept the result. The flaw is subtle (external validity, not internal validity).

reasons: Data contamination risk from same-corpus train/test splits is a real, documented, and quantifiable issue in code LM evaluation.
The distinction between semantic near-duplicates (forks, copies) and exact deduplication is correctly highlighted — function-name deduplication is insufficient.
The 8-gram overlap threshold (>50% overlap for >20% of test problems) is a concrete, operationalizable contamination criterion.
The three planted issues are well-separated: contamination risk, difficulty attribution, and in-distribution inflation are distinct causal mechanisms.
Difficulty is appropriately rated medium — the contamination explanation is known in the field but requires careful reasoning to articulate versus the simpler difficulty attribution.
notes: Contamination reasoning is a critical skill for LLM evaluation. The difficulty-attribution alternative is plausible enough to be a genuine distractor without being the correct explanation.

reasons: The second planted issue (wrong_optimization_target) contains a causal inaccuracy: the description states 'adding 3x more data typically increases model size and training time, which worsens inference latency'. Data volume affects training cost, not inference latency directly — inference latency is a function of model architecture and parameter count, not dataset size. A model retrained on 3x more data with the same architecture would have identical inference latency.

This inaccuracy could cause evaluators to penalize critique agents that correctly identify the scope issue but do not assert the data-latency relationship, or reward critique agents that incorrectly assert it.

The core scope_ambiguity_not_resolved issue and the case framing are sound and the case is worth keeping with the wrong_optimization_target issue description corrected to accurately reflect that the problem is the wrong optimization target was pursued, not that data volume mechanically harms latency.
The required empirical test and resolution thresholds are appropriate and do not inherit the inaccuracy.
notes: Revise the wrong_optimization_target issue description to remove the false causal claim about data volume and inference latency. The revised description should state that adding more data to retrain the same architecture leaves latency unchanged while failing to address the product need, and may worsen latency only if it triggers an architecture change (e.g., more layers, larger embeddings).

reasons: The prediction-vs-intervention conflation is a real, important, and commonly observed failure mode in ML project delivery.
Both planted issues are high-severity and logically distinct: prediction-intervention conflation is the conceptual error; no-intervention-design is the implementation gap that follows from it.
Mixed ground truth is correctly calibrated: the prediction model is genuinely valuable (AUC 0.85 is real signal), but the project goal required a closed loop.
The required empirical test (A/B test of intervention on model-identified at-risk users) is the correct resolution and directly tests whether the prediction model enables effective action.
Must-not-claim list (auc_0.85_is_insufficient, churn_model_is_overfit) prevents overclaiming that the predictive work is invalid.
notes: Defense partially wins in this case — the predictive work is genuinely valuable. Tests whether evaluators can hold 'correct component, insufficient scope' simultaneously without overclaiming.

reasons: Four planted issues are well-differentiated and cover distinct dimensions of the scope gap: safety underspecification, Bandit coverage limitation, sample representativeness, and overgeneralized conclusion.
The required empirical test (multi-dimensional rubric across correctness, fuzzing, semantic security, dependency audit, expert review) is realistic and maps directly to the scope gaps identified.
The correct_position of 'critique' is well-justified: no dimension of the evidence supports the 'safe for production' conclusion as stated.
Must-not-claim list (llm_code_is_never_safe, static_analysis_is_useless) correctly penalizes overclaiming in the opposite direction, testing defense_calibration.
The debate resolution thresholds are appropriately structured: critique wins if multi-dimensional evaluation reveals failures; defense wins only if all dimensions independently show high pass rates.
notes: The hardest scope-intent case. Tests multi-dimensional reasoning about safety evaluation. Correctly framed as a scope overreach problem, not a model quality problem.

reasons: False-positive trap is genuine: the imbalance-metric concern is a well-known and legitimate critique in general, but the dataset is explicitly documented as 50/50 balanced via stratified sampling, making the critique's entire premise false. The case is clearly defense_wins, not a partially-valid overclaim.
Defense rebuttal is specific and citable: the winning argument directly references the documented 50/50 stratified balance stated in the task prompt — a concrete, verifiable dataset property.

must_not_claim array correctly identifies the three claims a miscalibrated system would assert (class_imbalance_present, precision_is_misleading_on_this_dataset, recall_is_hidden_by_metric_choice), all of which are load-bearing false claims for scoring.

Asymmetry check passes: defense wins entirely from the provided dataset documentation — no new empirical data is required. The supports_critique_if condition requires a post-hoc audit contradicting the documentation, which is a context-changing edge case, not a gap in the base case.
acceptable_resolutions contains only ['defense_wins'] — no empirical_test_agreed or critique_wins fallback, consistent with the no-new-data-required defense.
notes: Clean false-positive trap for the most common metric-mismatch critique pattern. The 50/50 documentation is unambiguous and the defense argument is one-sentence. Serves as a competence floor check for defense_wins scoring: any system that still claims imbalance on a documented balanced dataset fails basic reading comprehension of the task prompt.

reasons: False-positive trap is genuine: the reviewer's critique applies a real academic evaluation norm (compare against same model class) to a context where that norm does not apply. The critique is not partially valid — it misidentifies the evaluation context entirely, confusing a production replacement decision with an architectural benchmark.

Defense rebuttal is specific and citable: the winning argument identifies the production deployment context and the business question as the basis for the baseline choice, citing the 15-point accuracy gap over the live production system as directly addressing the correct decision criterion.

must_not_claim array correctly identifies the three false claims a miscalibrated system would assert (rule_based_baseline_is_always_invalid, comparison_lacks_internal_validity, bert_baseline_required_for_valid_comparison), each of which would constitute a scoring false positive.

Asymmetry check passes: the production context is established in the task prompt itself ('compare it against the current production system') and no additional data is needed. The supports_defense_if condition references confirming the project brief, which is implicit in the task framing.
acceptable_resolutions contains only ['defense_wins']. The ambiguous_if condition (unclear project goal) is a legitimate scope qualifier, not a structural weakness in the case.
notes: Tests academic-vs-production evaluation context discrimination. The critique makes a methodologically sound argument in the wrong context. Difficulty is appropriately medium: the reviewer's argument is coherent and could mislead a system that applies evaluation norms without checking context. The supports_defense_if language could be slightly tightened to make the production context fully self-contained in the task prompt, but the existing framing is sufficient.

reasons: False-positive trap is genuine: the critique's concern (temporal generalizability unproven) is technically accurate as a general principle but is directly and explicitly addressed in the Limitations section quoted in the task prompt. The critique is not partially valid — it raises a disclosed constraint as if it were an undisclosed flaw.

Defense rebuttal is highly specific and citable: the Limitations section language is verbatim in the task prompt ('This model has been validated on transaction data from Q1 2024 only. Temporal generalizability has not been assessed. Re-validation is required before deployment in Q2 2024 or later quarters.') — the defense can quote exact text.

must_not_claim array correctly identifies the three false claims a miscalibrated system would assert (temporal_generalizability_is_unacknowledged, team_failed_to_disclose_scope_limitations, model_lacks_external_validity_without_qualification), all of which misrepresent the actual writeup.
Asymmetry check passes cleanly: the defense requires only reading the Limitations section, which is provided. No new data needed. This is the purest possible defense_wins case structure.

acceptable_resolutions contains only ['defense_wins']. The supports_critique_if condition (deployment recommendation contradicts the limitation) is a well-scoped edge case that would change the facts of the case, not a weakness in the base scenario.
notes: Canonical easy defense_wins case. Tests whether the protocol rewards appropriate epistemic humility in a writeup rather than penalizing it. The disclosed-limitation-as-critique pattern is a real and common false-positive failure mode in automated review systems. The Limitations section language is so explicit that any system producing a critique verdict here has a reading comprehension failure, making this a reliable competence floor check.

reasons: False-positive trap is genuine: the practical significance concern is a real and important issue in A/B testing, but the team explicitly quantified business value ($2M/0.1% = $6M/year for a 0.3% lift) in the Business Context section. The critique fails to engage with this information — it is not partially valid, it is a reading failure.

Defense rebuttal is highly specific and citable: the defense can reference the exact figures from the Business Context section ($2M per 0.1% lift, $6M/year for the observed 0.3% lift), making the practical significance determination a business judgment already resolved by the writeup.

must_not_claim array correctly identifies the three false claims a miscalibrated system would assert (practical_significance_is_unclear, effect_size_is_negligible, 0.3_percent_lift_is_not_economically_meaningful), all of which contradict the explicit economic quantification in the writeup.

Asymmetry check passes: the economic quantification is in the task prompt. Defense wins from provided information alone. The ambiguous_if condition (unverified revenue estimate + absent implementation costs) identifies a real edge case but one not present in the task as written — implementation costs are not raised by the critique being evaluated.

acceptable_resolutions contains only ['defense_wins']. Hard difficulty is well-calibrated: the Cohen's h argument in the ideal_critique is technically coherent and could mislead a system that processes statistical arguments without checking for business context sections.
notes: Hard defense_wins case with a sophisticated false-positive trap. The Cohen's h framing in the ideal_critique is deliberately chosen because it is statistically correct in isolation, making this a strong test of whether the system integrates business context rather than applying abstract statistical benchmarks. One minor observation: the ambiguous_if condition about unverified revenue estimates could apply to many real-world cases and slightly widens the theoretical ambiguity surface, but does not affect the case as scored against the specific critique being evaluated.
